# UC-13.1.14 · Long-Running Search Detection

## Description

Long-running searches consume shared resources and may indicate poorly written SPL or excessive time ranges.

## Value

Long-running searches consume shared resources and may indicate poorly written SPL or excessive time ranges.

## Implementation

Monitor search audit log for long-running searches (>10 minutes). Alert on searches consuming excessive resources. Identify optimization opportunities. Report on top resource-consuming searches weekly.

## Detailed Implementation

Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Monitoring Console.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `_internal` (scheduler, search audit log).
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Monitor search audit log for long-running searches (>10 minutes). Alert on searches consuming excessive resources. Identify optimization opportunities. Report on top resource-consuming searches weekly.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=_audit action=search info=completed
| where total_run_time > 600
| table _time, user, savedsearch_name, total_run_time, scan_count, event_count
| sort -total_run_time
```

Understanding this SPL

**Long-Running Search Detection** — Long-running searches consume shared resources and may indicate poorly written SPL or excessive time ranges.

Documented **Data sources**: `_internal` (scheduler, search audit log). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Monitoring Console. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: _audit.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=_audit. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• Filters the current rows with `where total_run_time > 600` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Pipeline stage (see **Long-Running Search Detection**): table _time, user, savedsearch_name, total_run_time, scan_count, event_count
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.


Step 3 — Validate
Run the search over calm and busy windows. Compare with the Splunk monitoring console and relevant health and introspection views, and spot-check key fields in sample events.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (long-running searches), Bar chart (top consumers by run time), Line chart (long search count trend).

## SPL

```spl
index=_audit action=search info=completed
| where total_run_time > 600
| table _time, user, savedsearch_name, total_run_time, scan_count, event_count
| sort -total_run_time
```

## Visualization

Table (long-running searches), Bar chart (top consumers by run time), Line chart (long search count trend).
